meta.title: General Multimodal Protein Design Enables DNA-Encoding of Chemistry

paragraphs.source: Evolution is an extraordinary engine for enzymatic diversity, yet the chemistry it has explored remains a narrow slice of what DNA can encode. Deep generative models can design new proteins that bind ligands, but none have created enzymes without pre-specifying catalytic residues. We introduce D ISCO, a multimodal model that co-designs protein sequence and 3D structure around arbitrary biomolecules, as well as inference-time scaling methods that optimize objectives across both modalities. Conditioned solely on reactive intermediates, D ISCO designs diverse heme enzymes with novel active-site geometries. These enzymes catalyze newto-nature carbene-transfer reactions, including alkene cyclopropanation, spirocyclopropanation, B–H, and C(sp3 )–H insertions, with high activities exceeding those of engineered enzymes. Random mutagenesis of a selected design further confirmed that enzyme activity can be improved through directed evolution. By providing a scalable route to evolvable enzymes, D ISCO broadens the potential scope of genetically encodable transformations.

(A carbene is a highly reactive carbon-containing species that can be transferred into new chemical bonds.)

sentences.plain: Random mutagenesis of a selected design further confirmed that enzyme activity can be improved through directed evolution. (Directed evolution means repeatedly mutating and screening proteins to find variants with better activity.)

sentences.source: By providing a scalable route to evolvable enzymes, D ISCO broadens the potential scope of genetically encodable transformations.
sentences.plain: By providing a scalable route to evolvable enzymes, DISCO broadens the potential scope of genetically encodable transformations.

paragraphs.source: Enzymes catalyze chemical reactions under mild conditions with high efficiency and specificity, underpinning applications in chemical manufacturing, drug synthesis, and emerging therapeutic modalities. [1, 2] Enzyme engineering has typically relied on iterative rounds of mutation and screening to accumulate beneficial sequence changes toward desired functions – an evolutionary process that can enable enzymes to perform functions not previously known in nature. [3] However, every such campaign requires an initial protein with measurable activity. For well-studied transformations, suitable starting points can be found among natural proteins or their close variants; but for many desirable new-to-nature chemistries, nature offers no obvious candidates. Identifying one remains largely a matter of chemical intuition – a laborious process fundamentally limited by what evolution has already sampled. How can we systematically design functional enzymes for chemical transformations that have no precedent in known biology? Generative deep learning models have transformed protein design, successfully enabling the creation of novel binders and the scaffolding of pre-defined structural motifs. [4–6] However, de novo design of enzymes for new-to-nature reactions remains largely unrealized. Current computational pipelines face two fundamental limitations. First, they rely heavily on pre-specified, fixed geometric arrangements of active-site residues or theozymes. [7–9] This precludes reactions for which no motif or precise mechanism is known and prevents the discovery of novel active-site geometries. Second, experimentally validated generative pipelines predominantly operate sequentially: they first generate a backbone and then devise a sequence to match the backbone design (inverse folding). [7, 10, 11] Because sequence and structure jointly determine function, this decoupled paradigm cannot leverage sequence-based objectives during critical stages of backbone formation, and vice versa. We introduce D ISCO (DIffusion for Sequence-structure CO-design), a general multimodal framework that simultaneously designs protein sequences and 3D structures de novo. D ISCO designs can be conditioned on and co-folded with arbitrary biomolecules, without pre-defined residue motifs. A novel multimodal Feynman-Kac corrector method allows D ISCO to optimize sequence and structure objectives together. D ISCO achieves state-of-the-art in silico results in generating binders for diverse biomolecular targets with fine-grained property control. Applied to new-to-nature catalysis experimentally, D ISCO generates diverse, evolvable carbene transferases with novel active sites. No inverse-folding is required. These enzymes have activities that surpass typical starting points for directed evolution and, in selected cases, extensively evolved variants. Ultimately, D ISCO enables the discovery of biocatalysts and protein motifs previously unknown to biology, expanding the searchable space of DNA-encoded chemical reactivity.

paragraphs.source: D ISCO models discrete sequences and 3D structures as a joint distribution that is denoised through a unified generative process (Fig. 1). We achieve this by training a single deep neural network that employs a masked discrete diffusion process for sequences and, in parallel, leverages continuous diffusion for 3D atomic coordinates. By independently sampling noise per modality during training, one can provably learn the joint reverse process using only unimodal diffusion losses [12]: the standard denoising score matching loss for coordinates and the masked diffusion language model loss for sequences. We trained D ISCO using only data from the Protein ·· m m m m ·· ·· m E m A ·· ·· S E D K ·· Data Bank without special filtering, avoiding any selection biases introduced when models are trained exclusively on “designable” structures that folding algorithms can successfully predict. D ISCO’s architecture comprises an input embedder that produces single and pair representations of all input modalities, a frozen protein language model (pLM) that encodes partial sequences, [13] a Pairformer stack that contextualizes these representations, and a joint diffusion module built on atom-level attention that predicts both sequence logits and denoised coordinates of the backbone (Fig. 1B). [14] We inject SE(3) symmetries softly through data augmentation rather than architectural constraints. Central to our approach is a cross-modal recycling mechanism that conditions each generseq ation step on four distinct encodings: the model’s current predicted clean sequence x̂0 and seq structure x̂0struct , as well as the current noised sequence xτ and structure xτstruct . We train a structure encoder [15] and employ the pLM to inject sequence information. This bidirectional conditioning ensures that sequence predictions are informed by emerging structural features while structural predictions adapt to evolving sequence identity. D ISCO’s inference strategy proved critical to generation quality (Fig. 2B, Fig. S4). Standard masked diffusion—iteratively unmasking tokens without revision [16]—yields sequences that fail to fold into their generated geometries. By enabling self-correction during sequence inference [17, 18] and introducing a novel sequence temperature mechanism that smooths the amino acid distribution for overconfident tokens early in the trajectory, we drastically improve co-designability. Combining this with noisy guidance [12] allows D ISCO to achieve state-of-the-art performance in sequence-structure co-design. On unconditional monomer design, approximately 90% of generated sequences refold to within 2 Å RMSD of their designed backbone structures using ESMFold (Fig. 2A, Fig. S5, Table S4). [19] Compared to baselines, D ISCO achieves the highest sequence and structure diversity and novelty without sacrificing co-designability (Fig. 2A).

paragraphs.source: D ISCO flexibly conditions on arbitrary biomolecular contexts, including small molecules, metallocofactors, reactive intermediates, and nucleic acids. Non-protein molecules are represented identically to proteins – with discrete tokens for chemical identity and corresponding atomic coordinates. The model simultaneously unmasks protein sequences while denoising the coordinates of targets ranging from small molecules and metal cofactors to reactive intermediates and nucleic acids. These contexts co-fold with the designed protein throughout the generative trajectory: D ISCO adjusts the coordinates of conditioning biomolecules while designing the protein, capturing conformational changes induced by molecular interactions and extending generation beyond fixed, pre-defined atomic scaffolds. To benchmark this capability, we designed a new benchmark, S TUDIO -179, a library of 179 natural and non-natural ligands spanning applications in catalysis, pharmaceuticals, luminescence, and sensing. The library encompasses rigid molecules (e.g., the pollutant tetrachlorodibenzodioxin), large or flexible molecules (e.g., the cofactor CoQ10 ), and metals/metalloclusters (e.g., [4Fe-4S]). For each ligand, we quantify the fraction of generated designs that are both structurally diverse and co-designable, defining co-designable as when the protein backbone and all ligand centroids have an RMSD < 2 Å upon refolding with Chai-1. [20] Compared to baselines, D ISCO generates the highest proportion of diverse, co-designable complexes for 178 of 179 evaluated ligands (Figs. 2C, 2D, Figs. S10, S11, S25). Our formulation naturally supports multi-context conditioning—such as designing proteins to interface with multiple distinct ligands simultaneously—again yielding high-quality complexes (Fig. 2D). Extending beyond small molecules, we evaluated D ISCO’s performance on macromolecular interfaces. D ISCO successfully generated co-designable proteins predicted to bind to both sequence-specific DNA and RNA sequences, outperforming existing models (Fig. 2C). Note that because the frozen pLM in D ISCO was not trained on multimeric complexes, we constrained our current evaluation to non-protein-binding tasks.

paragraphs.source: Figure 2. D ISCO generates diverse, designable protein sequences and structures across a wide variety of functions evaluated in silico. (A) In unconditional monomer generation, D ISCO outperforms existing methods in terms of co-designability, novelty, and diversity (400 samples). (B) Ablation of unconditional monomer generation with D ISCO to identify best inference-time practices. (C) D ISCO outperforms existing methods for conditional generation across diverse targets, including natural and non-natural molecules, multi-ligands, DNA, and RNA. (D) Examples of designed (blue) and refolded (white) protein complexes show high agreement. (E) FKC-MM enables the joint steering of sequence and structure, as demonstrated by two multimodal rewards: disulfide bridges and cation-π interactions. Example generated proteins with high disulfide bridge counts are shown at right. (F) FKC-SG generates more proteins with predicted binding specificity (a large separation between binding sites of on- and off-targets) compared to existing filtering methods. Example refolded structures are shown at right (on-target in pink, off-target in yellow).

paragraphs.sectionTitle: Multimodal Inference Steering Using Feynman-Kac Correctors
paragraphs.source: The multimodality of D ISCO enables inference-time scaling with both sequence and structure signals (Fig. 1C). Rather than relying on inefficient generate-and-filter (best-of-N) approaches, which often fail when desired traits are rare, we utilize the Feynman-Kac Corrector (FKC) framework to incorporate target properties into the generation process directly and tilt sample distributions toward having desirable traits without necessarily invoking expensive reward oracles. [21, 22] We derive two novel FKC approaches. First, FKC - Multimodal (FKC-MM) allows for reward tilting with functions defined jointly over both discrete sequences and continuous structures. When using multimodal reward functions for increasing disulfide bond content or cation-π interactions, FKC-MM generates samples with enriched target properties compared to structure-only FKC or unconditional generations (Fig. 2E). Notably, while unconditional generation underrepresents cysteines (Fig. S6), the top 2% of designable, 100-amino acid proteins produced by FKC-MM contain six disulfide bonds – a density matched by only the top 0.2% of comparable training proteins (Fig. S9). These results underscore the benefit of being able to generate sequences alongside protein structures. We further introduce FKC - Specificity Guidance (FKC-SG) to steer D ISCO towards binding exclusively to a target molecule while avoiding a structurally similar decoy. This is achieved by sampling from a tilted distribution that encourages samples that are likely under the on-target model while penalizing samples that are likely under the off-target model (Section A.7). We evaluated FKC-SG on discriminating two dissimilar molecules (e.g., biotin from pyridoxal 5’-phosphate (PLP)), as well as very similar molecules, such as aldosterone from cortisone (two constitutional isomeric steroids) and valine from proline (two hydrophobic, aliphatic amino acids). We find that FKC-SG generates proteins that have high separation between on- and off-target complexes (Fig. 2F), improving upon filters like best-of-N, which in some cases do not produce any hits passing in silico filters.

sentences.source: The multimodality of D ISCO enables inference-time scaling with both sequence and structure signals (Fig. 1C).
sentences.plain: The multimodality of DISCO enables inference-time scaling with both sequence and structure signals (Figure 1C).

sentences.source: Rather than relying on inefficient generate-and-filter (best-of-N) approaches, which often fail when desired traits are rare, we utilize the Feynman-Kac Corrector (FKC) framework to incorporate target properties into the generation process directly and tilt sample distributions toward having desirable traits without necessarily invoking expensive reward oracles. [21, 22] We derive two novel FKC approaches.

[21, 22] We derive two novel FKC approaches.

paragraphs.sectionTitle: DISCO Designs Exhibit Realistic Protein Features with Novel, Complementary Motifs
paragraphs.source: D ISCO’s designs capture the complex statistical properties of proteins. In unconditional generation, samples display natural amino-acid compositions (Fig. 3A, Fig. S6), diverse secondary structures (Fig. 3B, Fig. S7), favorable local Ramachandran geometries (Fig. 3C), reasonable radii of gyration (Fig. S8), and native-like surface properties including appropriate hydrophobicity and net charge (Fig. 3D). The designs further exhibit high long-range contact order (Fig. 3E), indicating complex, well-connected topologies rather than trivial folds often seen in de novo designs. [23] These properties extend to conditional generation (Figs. S12–S17).

sentences.plain: DISCO’s designs capture the complex statistical properties of proteins.

paragraphs.sectionTitle: DISCO Designs Exhibit Realistic Protein Features with Novel, Complementary Motifs
paragraphs.source: RMSD5-residue = 5.04 Å to Q76IH4 We hypothesized that jointly generating sequence and structure allows the designed binding site to adapt its chemistry and geometry as the target adjusts its conformation, yielding a mutually compatible interface. This is a capability unique to co-design, allowing D ISCO to construct chemically appropriate microenvironments without inverse folding or any pre-specified residue motif. Indeed, ligand-conditioned generation yields pockets that are quantitatively responsive to ligand identity: binding-site lipophilicity correlates with ligand hydrophobicity (Fig. 3I, Figs. S20, S21), appropriate coordinating residues emerge for specific cofactors (Fig. 3J, Fig. S27), and sufficiently large cavities form to avoid steric clashes (Fig. S19). Prior co-design models capture these physiochemical trends less consistently (Fig. S22), which aligns with their lower co-designability and diversity. Furthermore, D ISCO samples valid ligand conformers beyond the reference input (Figs. 3K–M, Figs. S17, S26), by selectively exploring rotatable bonds while preserving rigid geometries. These complementary pockets generated by D ISCO exhibit striking novelty and diversity. For coordination spheres up to ten residues, motif similarity searches using Folddisco [24] show that most binding motifs in co-designable generations lack natural homologs in AlphaFoldDB (novel motif defined as no match or RMSD > 3 Å; Figs. 3F, 3G, Fig. S28). Clustering the generated motifs via pairwise distance shows over 90% cluster diversity (Figs. S23, S24). D ISCO also generates globally novel sequences, and many folds have no close homologs in the Protein Data Bank (Fig. 3H). Such novelty and diversity are only relevant when the motifs are foldable and physicochemically realistic – standards D ISCO meet while expanding well beyond the space of known global scaffolds and local motifs.

paragraphs.source: Enzyme design is among the most challenging applications for conditional protein generation, especially for new-to-nature catalytic activities that lie outside the model’s training distribution. Carbene transfer reactions, unexplored in known natural enzymes, have proven valuable for expanding the catalytic repertoire of biology. [25] While directed evolution of cytochrome P450, cytochrome c, and various globin variants has produced numerous carbene transferases, identifying initial activity for a desired reaction remains challenging and laborious, usually limited to screening a limited set of known scaffolds. Previous computational approaches have designed heme enzymes for only a handful of carbene reactions using physics-based theozymes and pre-specified active-site residue geometries [26–28]—a strategy that is expensive, biased by the choice of motif, and difficult to generalize, especially when the catalytic mechanism is not known. We hypothesized that D ISCO’s ability to co-generate sequence and structure around arbitrary molecules could bypass residue motif or theozyme specification. Carbene-transfer reactions proceed via formation of an iron–carbenoid intermediate, most often generated by nitrogen extrusion from a diazo precursor. This intermediate subsequently transfers the carbene fragment to a substrate through diverse pathways, including alkene cyclopropanation, or insertion into C–H, B–H, Si–H, or N–H bonds. [25, 29] Formation of the heme–carbene intermediate is generally the rate-determining step (>20 kcal/mol, Fig. S3). [30] Therefore, we conditioned D ISCO on DFT-derived geometries and bonding patterns of the hemecarbene precursor complex rather than on a complete transition-state model. This choice reflects a deliberate simplification: rather than fixing an exact transition state calculated in vacuo, we let D ISCO’s co-folding mechanism sample conformations of the reactive intermediate that would be compatible with the protein it is constructing. This allows us to bypass the theozyme-based design entirely, where the limited mechanistic understanding for new-to-nature reactions may preclude the design of an accurate theozyme. D ISCO is free to explore catalytic solutions without being constrained by human assumptions about the protein residues required for activity or a fixed transition state geometry. From ∼ 104 in silico generated sequence-structure pairs – one to two orders of magnitude fewer than recent structure-based pipelines [7, 10] – we applied a computational filter (Fig. 1D) combining predicted structures (AlphaFold 3 [14] and Chai-1 [20]), confidence measures such as chain pAE and ipTM, active-site contact counts, solvent exposure, net charge, and exposed hydrophobicity, yielding 90 designs for experimental testing (Section A.10). Neither the sequences nor the structures were redesigned after generation. Computational analysis confirms that the designed active sites represent new residue geometries (Fig. 3F, >80% with >3 Å RMSD in the five nearest residues). Moreover, while some designs closely recapitulate the reference geometry (RMSD < 1 Å), a substantial fraction adopt more divergent conformations (RMSD > 2 Å), primarily through rotations about the iron–carbenoid bond (Figs. 3L, 3M). This indicates that D ISCO actively explores conformational space even for reaction intermediates. The designed enzymes are highly diverse: no pair shares more than 50% sequence identity, and the 90 designs form 75 distinct structural clusters (TM-score < 0.5). To assess the catalytic generality, we selected four substrates spanning distinct carbene transfer reactions: p-methoxystyrene (1a) with ethyl diazoacetate (EDA, 5a) for cyclopropanation, [31] 1,3-dimethylimidazol-2-ylidene borane (2a) with ethyl 2-diazopropanoate (EDP, 5b) for B–H insertion, [32] 1-phenylpyrrolidine (3a) with 5a for C(sp3 )–H insertion, [33] and tert-butyl-3methyleneazetidine-1-carboxylate (4a) with 5a for spirocyclopropanation of a pharmaceutically relevant scaffold. [34] All 90 designs were screened in whole-cell format in Escherichia coli across all four reactions. To control for background reactivity from endogenous hemoproteins, we overexpressed an unrelated protein (TrpB from Thermotoga maritima) under identical conditions. Top hits were selected for validation (Fig. 4). Several reactions exhibited high background activity from endogenous E. coli hemoproteins, which complicated identification of lower-activity designs, especially since overexpressing these designs may disrupt heme homeostasis and further reduce their activity. We note that hemin alone has no detectable activity for the spirocyclopropanation or the C(sp3 )–H insertion reaction. Remarkably, screening across our selected carbene reactions confirmed that the designed variants were not only functional (2.2 – 66% exceeded the TmTrpB control) but, in several cases, exhibited high catalytic activity. For the cyclopropanation of p-methoxystyrene, the best design achieved 72% yield and a total turnover number (TTN) of 4,050 with a 99:1 diastereomeric ratio (Fig. S32), surpassing both early evolved P411 enzymes and the recently reported designed enzyme PNC2 which scaffolded a helix bundle around a porphyrin-based theozyme (Fig. 4A). [27, 31] For B–H insertion, the top variant achieved 98% yield and 5,170 TTN (Fig. 4B), far exceeding both the prior starting point (120 TTN) and the laboratory-evolved variant (2490 TTN). [32] Most notably, for C(sp3 )–H insertion – a highly challenging transformation whose previous engineering campaign required 14 rounds of directed evolution and for which mechanistic uncertainty precludes theozyme construction – our best design reached 42% yield and 2,360 TTN for the alkylation of 1-phenylpyrrolidine, rivaling the performance of previously evolved P411-CHF catalysts on this substrate (2,030 TTN) (Fig. 4C). [33] The sterically and Evolved P450H2-5-F10: 364 TTN Theozyme-based design PNC2: 630 TTN Hemin: 80 TTN Rma cyt c: 120 TTN Evolved Rma cyt c: 2490 TTN RMSD5-residue = 7.40 Å to Q2JM39 electronically demanding spirocyclopropanation proved more challenging, [34] yielding fewer active variants with modest activity. Although enantioselectivity was not a design criterion, modest enantioselectivities were observed across all reactions, with enantiomeric excesses up to 35% for the spirocyclopropanation (Figs. S29–S31). Notably, enzymes with a preference for either enantiomer were identified for three reactions. To probe the evolvability of D ISCO’s designs, we selected the spirocyclopropanation reaction, where design alone had yielded only modest activity. We subjected the designed carbene transferase dCT-H11 to a single round of error-prone PCR and screened approximately 700 mutants (Fig. 5B). Around 35 variants displayed noticeably improved activity relative to the parent enzyme with divergent enantioselectivity, either enhancing the parent’s preference (from +35% to +49% ee) or inverting it entirely (+35% to -35% ee, Fig. S33). The top variants harbor substitutions scattered across the protein, consistent with the long-range epistatic effects characteristic of natural enzyme evolution. These results suggest that D ISCO’s designs can occupy regions of sequence space with accessible uphill paths for further optimization. The top hits span diverse folds with novel motifs (Fig. 5). The closest structural match to dCT-H11 is a TetR-family transcription factor from the extremophile Haloarcula marismortui (PDB ID: 3CRJ, TM-score: 0.81) that lacks known catalytic activity. dCT-H11 shares only 21% sequence identity with 3CRJ, suggesting that D ISCO repurposed a topology not pre-specified for catalysis. The most similar residue motif to the five closest active-site residues, drawn from AlphaFoldDB, shows a large mismatch (RMSD > 7 Å), confirming that the geometry of the catalytic residues is itself novel. Other designs are even more divergent: dCT-F9 (closest match: 5MSV, TM-score 0.52, 5% sequence identity) and dCT-G9 (closest match: 7C7E, TMscore 0.51, 9% sequence identity) adopt folds that are substantially more remote from known structures, with no corresponding motif identified in AlphaFoldDB based on their five nearest active-site residues (Fig. 5C). Notably, none of the closest structural matches correspond to naturally heme-binding proteins, highlighting the ability of the model to capture underlying biochemical principles and repurpose unrelated protein folds for heme binding and carbene transfer reactions. Together, these results demonstrate that the model can create novel proteins with emergent functions, extending beyond the boundaries of natural protein space.

sentences.plain: To assess the catalytic generality, we selected four substrates spanning distinct carbene transfer reactions: p-methoxystyrene (1a) with ethyl diazoacetate (EDA, 5a) for cyclopropanation, [31] 1,3-dimethylimidazol-2-ylidene borane (2a) with ethyl 2-diazopropanoate (EDP, 5b) for B–H insertion, [32] 1-phenylpyrrolidine (3a) with 5a for C(sp3 )–H insertion, [33] and tert-butyl-3methyleneazetidine-1-carboxylate (4a) with 5a for spirocyclopropanation of a pharmaceutically relevant scaffold. [34] All 90 designs were screened in whole-cell format in Escherichia coli across all four reactions.

sentences.source: To control for background reactivity from endogenous hemoproteins, we overexpressed an unrelated protein (TrpB from Thermotoga maritima) under identical conditions.
sentences.plain: To control for background reactivity from endogenous hemoproteins, we overexpressed an unrelated protein (TrpB from Thermotoga maritima) under identical conditions.

sentences.source: Top hits were selected for validation (Fig. 4).
sentences.plain: Top hits were selected for validation (Figure 4).

sentences.source: Several reactions exhibited high background activity from endogenous E. coli hemoproteins, which complicated identification of lower-activity designs, especially since overexpressing these designs may disrupt heme homeostasis and further reduce their activity.
sentences.plain: Several reactions exhibited high background activity from endogenous E. coli hemoproteins, which complicated identification of lower-activity designs, especially since overexpressing these designs may disrupt heme homeostasis and further reduce their activity.

sentences.source: We note that hemin alone has no detectable activity for the spirocyclopropanation or the C(sp3 )–H insertion reaction.
sentences.plain: We note that hemin alone has no detectable activity for the spirocyclopropanation or the C(sp3 )–H insertion reaction.

sentences.source: Remarkably, screening across our selected carbene reactions confirmed that the designed variants were not only functional (2.2 – 66% exceeded the TmTrpB control) but, in several cases, exhibited high catalytic activity.
sentences.plain: Remarkably, screening across our selected carbene reactions confirmed that the designed variants were not only functional (2.2 – 66% exceeded the TmTrpB control) but, in several cases, exhibited high catalytic activity.

sentences.source: For the cyclopropanation of p-methoxystyrene, the best design achieved 72% yield and a total turnover number (TTN) of 4,050 with a 99:1 diastereomeric ratio (Fig. S32), surpassing both early evolved P411 enzymes and the recently reported designed enzyme PNC2 which scaffolded a helix bundle around a porphyrin-based theozyme (Fig. 4A). [27, 31] For B–H insertion, the top variant achieved 98% yield and 5,170 TTN (Fig. 4B), far exceeding both the prior starting point (120 TTN) and the laboratory-evolved variant (2490 TTN). [32] Most notably, for C(sp3 )–H insertion – a highly challenging transformation whose previous engineering campaign required 14 rounds of directed evolution and for which mechanistic uncertainty precludes theozyme construction – our best design reached 42% yield and 2,360 TTN for the alkylation of 1-phenylpyrrolidine, rivaling the performance of previously evolved P411-CHF catalysts on this substrate (2,030 TTN) (Fig. 4C). [33] The sterically and Evolved P450H2-5-F10: 364 TTN Theozyme-based design PNC2: 630 TTN Hemin: 80 TTN Rma cyt c: 120 TTN Evolved Rma cyt c: 2490 TTN RMSD5-residue = 7.40 Å to Q2JM39 electronically demanding spirocyclopropanation proved more challenging, [34] yielding fewer active variants with modest activity.
sentences.plain: For the cyclopropanation of p-methoxystyrene, the best design achieved 72% yield and a total turnover number (TTN) of 4,050 with a 99:1 diastereomeric ratio (Figure S32), surpassing both early evolved P411 enzymes and the recently reported designed enzyme PNC2 which scaffolded a helix bundle around a porphyrin-based theozyme (Figure 4A). [27, 31] For B–H insertion, the top variant achieved 98% yield and 5,170 TTN (Figure 4B), far exceeding both the prior starting point (120 TTN) and the laboratory-evolved variant (2490 TTN). [32] Most notably, for C(sp3 )–H insertion – a highly challenging transformation whose previous engineering campaign required 14 rounds of directed evolution and for which mechanistic uncertainty precludes theozyme construction – our best design reached 42% yield and 2,360 TTN for the alkylation of 1-phenylpyrrolidine, rivaling the performance of previously evolved P411-CHF catalysts on this substrate (2,030 TTN) (Figure 4C). [33] The sterically and Evolved P450H2-5-F10: 364 TTN Theozyme-based design PNC2: 630 TTN Hemin: 80 TTN Rma cyt c: 120 TTN Evolved Rma cyt c: 2490 TTN RMSD5-residue = 7.40 Å to Q2JM39 electronically demanding spirocyclopropanation proved more challenging, [34] yielding fewer active variants with modest activity. (TTN, total turnover number, counts how many product molecules one catalyst molecule makes before stopping.)

sentences.source: Although enantioselectivity was not a design criterion, modest enantioselectivities were observed across all reactions, with enantiomeric excesses up to 35% for the spirocyclopropanation (Figs. S29–S31).
sentences.plain: Although enantioselectivity was not a design criterion, modest enantioselectivities were observed across all reactions, with enantiomeric excesses up to 35% for the spirocyclopropanation (Figures S29–S31). (Enantioselectivity means preferring one mirror-image form of a molecule over the other.)

sentences.source: Notably, enzymes with a preference for either enantiomer were identified for three reactions.
sentences.plain: Notably, enzymes with a preference for either enantiomer were identified for three reactions.

sentences.source: To probe the evolvability of D ISCO’s designs, we selected the spirocyclopropanation reaction, where design alone had yielded only modest activity.
sentences.plain: To probe the evolvability of DISCO’s designs, we selected the spirocyclopropanation reaction, where design alone had yielded only modest activity.

sentences.source: We subjected the designed carbene transferase dCT-H11 to a single round of error-prone PCR and screened approximately 700 mutants (Fig. 5B).
sentences.plain: We subjected the designed carbene transferase dCT-H11 to a single round of error-prone PCR and screened approximately 700 mutants (Figure 5B).

sentences.source: Around 35 variants displayed noticeably improved activity relative to the parent enzyme with divergent enantioselectivity, either enhancing the parent’s preference (from +35% to +49% ee) or inverting it entirely (+35% to -35% ee, Fig. S33).
sentences.plain: Around 35 variants displayed noticeably improved activity relative to the parent enzyme with divergent enantioselectivity, either enhancing the parent’s preference (from +35% to +49% ee) or inverting it entirely (+35% to -35% ee, Figure S33).

sentences.source: The top variants harbor substitutions scattered across the protein, consistent with the long-range epistatic effects characteristic of natural enzyme evolution.
sentences.plain: The top variants harbor substitutions scattered across the protein, consistent with the long-range epistatic effects characteristic of natural enzyme evolution.

sentences.source: These results suggest that D ISCO’s designs can occupy regions of sequence space with accessible uphill paths for further optimization.
sentences.plain: These results suggest that DISCO’s designs can occupy regions of sequence space with accessible uphill paths for further optimization.

sentences.source: The top hits span diverse folds with novel motifs (Fig. 5).
sentences.plain: The top hits span diverse folds with novel motifs (Figure 5).

sentences.source: The closest structural match to dCT-H11 is a TetR-family transcription factor from the extremophile Haloarcula marismortui (PDB ID: 3CRJ, TM-score: 0.81) that lacks known catalytic activity. dCT-H11 shares only 21% sequence identity with 3CRJ, suggesting that D ISCO repurposed a topology not pre-specified for catalysis.
sentences.plain: The closest structural match to dCT-H11 is a TetR-family transcription factor from the extremophile Haloarcula marismortui (PDB ID: 3CRJ, TM-score: 0.81) that lacks known catalytic activity. dCT-H11 shares only 21% sequence identity with 3CRJ, suggesting that DISCO repurposed a topology not pre-specified for catalysis.

sentences.source: The most similar residue motif to the five closest active-site residues, drawn from AlphaFoldDB, shows a large mismatch (RMSD > 7 Å), confirming that the geometry of the catalytic residues is itself novel.
sentences.plain: The most similar residue motif to the five closest active-site residues, drawn from AlphaFoldDB, shows a large mismatch (RMSD > 7 Å), confirming that the geometry of the catalytic residues is itself novel.

sentences.source: Other designs are even more divergent: dCT-F9 (closest match: 5MSV, TM-score 0.52, 5% sequence identity) and dCT-G9 (closest match: 7C7E, TMscore 0.51, 9% sequence identity) adopt folds that are substantially more remote from known structures, with no corresponding motif identified in AlphaFoldDB based on their five nearest active-site residues (Fig. 5C).
sentences.plain: Other designs are even more divergent: dCT-F9 (closest match: 5MSV, TM-score 0.52, 5% sequence identity) and dCT-G9 (closest match: 7C7E, TMscore 0.51, 9% sequence identity) adopt folds that are much more remote from known structures, with no corresponding motif identified in AlphaFoldDB based on their five nearest active-site residues (Figure 5C).

sentences.source: Notably, none of the closest structural matches correspond to naturally heme-binding proteins, highlighting the ability of the model to capture underlying biochemical principles and repurpose unrelated protein folds for heme binding and carbene transfer reactions.
sentences.plain: Notably, none of the closest structural matches correspond to naturally heme-binding proteins, highlighting the ability of the model to capture underlying biochemical principles and repurpose unrelated protein folds for heme binding and carbene transfer reactions.

sentences.source: Together, these results demonstrate that the model can create novel proteins with emergent functions, extending beyond the boundaries of natural protein space.
sentences.plain: Together, these results shows that the model can create novel proteins with emergent functions, extending beyond the boundaries of natural protein space.

paragraphs.sectionTitle: General Biomolecular Design Unlocks New-to-Nature Reactivities
paragraphs.source: These results establish a general platform for functional protein design in which sequence and 3D structure are generated as a coupled object and flexibly conditioned on any biomolecules. Computationally, careful inference alignment across modalities via cross-modal recycling, self-correction, and entropy regularization enables D ISCO to generate diverse, high-quality proteins that outperform existing co-generation methods, while inference-time steering on both sequence and structure turns generative sampling into a controllable search process. Experimentally, conditioning only on reaction intermediates was sufficient to produce promiscuous carbene transferases across four new-to-nature reactions, bypassing transition-state calculations, theozyme scaffolding, and extensive wet-lab screening. From just 90 genes, top designs achieved 98% yield for B–H insertion and >2,300 TTN for C(sp3 )–H insertion, exceeding previously evolved biocatalysts. Importantly, the designed active-site residues are novel, exhibit stereoselectivity, and occupy accessible fitness landscapes that support subsequent evolution. The ability to genetically encode arbitrary chemical transformations requires global discovery with local optimization. D ISCO provides a global search for diverse, evolvable enzymes. Yet the reactions explored here represent only a small subset of synthetically valuable transformations not found in nature. Mastering increasingly complex mechanisms likely requires closing the loop between generative discovery and directed evolution, informed by richer biophysical constraints. More broadly, co-designing proteins with arbitrary biomolecular partners charts a path toward programmable biological systems whose complexity rivals that of natural evolution, and whose function serves human intent.

paragraphs.sectionTitle: A In Silico Methods and Results

paragraphs.source: set. We designate the final element of this set as a specialized mask token V = m, whereas the remaining V − 1 elements in V form the set of natural amino acids, including one unknown amino acid UNK. We use the diffusion time convention where time τ = 0 is associated with the data distribution pdata and τ = 1 is associated with pnoise . We describe proteins with L residues with backbone coordinates xstruct and sequence xseq as x = ( xstruct , xseq ) ∈ (X struct , X seq ) := (R L×4×3 , V L ) = X where X denotes the space of protein backbone coordinates with associated amino acid types. However, for simplicity, whenever it is clear from context, we also omit superscripts struct and seq to avoid notational clutter. We use τ to denote inference time, with t(τ ) to denote structure time and r (τ ) to denote sequence time. We also use the shorthand t to denote t(τ ) and r to denote r (τ ). simplex. Each point on u ∈ ∆d corresponds to a categorical distribution Cat( j; u) = u j for j ∈ V . Let δ( x ) ∈ ∆d be the one-hot distribution that places all its mass on x. The data distribution pdata is provided as an empirical distribution on n sequences in the form of a training set D = { x }n ⊂ X . We denote for x ∈ V , δ( x ) ∈ ∆d given by Cat( j; δ( x )) = 1 if j = x and 0 otherwise. Finally, we reserve superscripts for set indexing purposes, e.g. xi , i ∈ [d], while seq subscripts are used to represent positions in time of a discrete sample xr , r ∈ [0, 1].

paragraphs.source: For a multi-modal space where elements contain multiple components, we describe the theoretical framework for modeling such systems, first individually, then in combination.

sentences.source: For a multi-modal space where elements contain multiple components, we describe the theoretical framework for modeling such systems, first individually, then in combination.
sentences.plain: For a multi-modal space where elements contain multiple components, we describe the theoretical method for modeling such systems, first individually, then in combination.

paragraphs.source: Generative modeling in continuous space systems can be formulated as the simulation of a Stochastic Differential Equation (SDE) forward in time to destroy information, then reverse in time to generate new samples from the target distribution. In particular, during training, information is gradually destroyed from the training dataset by simulation with samples x ∼ pdata ( x ) using the following SDE: dxt = f t ( xt )dt + gt dWt , xt=0 ∼ pdata ( x ) where f t ( xt ) : [0, 1] × X struct → X struct is generally some pre-defined linear drift, Wt is the standard Wiener process, gt ∈ R≥0 is the diffusion coefficient, a scaling factor for the noise. The drift f t and the diffusion gt are chosen such that at pt=1 ≈ N (0, I ). Given this forward process, a reverse-time denoising SDE can be defined in the opposite direction,  dxt = − f t ( xt ) + gt2 ∇ log pt ( xt ) dt + gt dWt , xt=1 ∼ N (0, I ) (S2) where pt is the marginal distribution induced by the stochastic process in (S1). By training a model of the score function ∇ log pt ( xt ), the SDE can be reversed to generate points approxi(Back to Table of Contents) mately from the data distribution. This leads to a natural parameterization using a denoising network Dθ ( xt , t) which can be used to generate new samples using the following reverse-time denoising SDE:  dxt = D ( xt , t) − xt − f t ( xt ) + gt2 θ σt2 xt=1 ∼ N (0, I ) Rt where σt2 = 0 gr2 dr represents the total noise of the system at time t. It is possible to learn Dθ ( xt ) using the standard denoising score matching loss [35]: Et,x0 ∼ pdata ,xt ∼ pt (xt | x0 ) 1 ∥ Dθ ( xt , t) − x0 ∥22 σt2

paragraphs.source: In the discrete-space setting, we require a slightly different treatment of discrete variables. We follow the discrete diffusion literature, which defines a similar noising and denoising process based on a masking process. In this case, continuous-time Markov chains (CTMC) or jump processes describe transitions on the discrete state spaces. In this section, we focus on the discrete time setting where time is divided up into T sub-intervals such that r̃ ∈ [0, T ) represents normalized time r := r̃/T. This process can also be described in the fully continuoustime setting, which requires more rigorous treatment and can be found in related works such as [12, 17]. We focus on “masked” discrete diffusion modeling where information is destroyed by transitioning to a special mask token m independently for each xri ̸= m: L i =1 i =1 pr ( xr | x0 ) = ∏ pt ( xri | x0i ) = ∏ Cat( xri ; αr δ( x0i ) + (1 − αr )δ(m)). where αr describes the noise schedule similarly to gt in the continuous case, and is specified such that α0 = 1 and α1 = 0. This describes a process where at time r a fraction αr of the tokens are in their original state and 1 − αr of the tokens are in the masked state. The simplest conditional reverse transition kernel for a token xri is described by:  Cat( xi ; δ( xi )) r  r −1  qt ( xri −1 | xri , x0i ) = i Cat xi ; (1−αr−1 )δ(m)+(αr−1 −αr )δ(x0 ) r −1 1− αr xri ̸= m xri = m.

paragraphs.source: This leads to a natural parameterization for the transitions of a discrete time generative model. We first define a (possibly) time-dependent denoiser network Dr,θ : V L × [0, 1] → ∆ L that predicts the probabilities of a clean sample x0 ∼ Dr,θ ( xr ):   Cat( xri −1 ; δ( xri ))   i i i i (x ) pr,θ ( xr−1 | xr , Dr,θ ( xr )) = (1−αr−1 )δ(m)+(αr−1 −αt ) Dr,θ r i  Cat xr−1 ; 1− αr xri ̸= m xri = m. where Dθi represents the predicted distribution over tokens for the i-th index of the sequence. Using this reverse parameterization, as T → ∞, there exists an evidence lower bound (ELBO) to the log likelihood of the data distribution:   L dαr i  ∑ δ( x0i )T log Dr,θ · E ( xr ) dr dr 1 − αr xr ∼pr (·| x0 ) i=1,x i =m This can be used to train Dθ using standard stochastic gradient-descent type algorithms. It can be thought of as training a masked discrete diffusion model by optimizing a weighted 1 r cross-entropy loss over masking levels αr weighted by dα dr · 1−αr . However, there exist several major limitations of the denoising process in (S6), which is that as T → ∞, 1. Lack of Order Control. An analytic Gillespie-style sampler [36] shows that the denoising proceeds by uniformly randomly selecting a masked position, offering no control over the generation order of tokens. 2. Token Commitment. (S7) reveals that once unmasked, a specific token is committed to and never revisited. This means that if “mistakes” are made in the generation process, they can never be corrected. These two issues have led to a variety of solutions involving alternative reverse-time processes for discrete generation. We use a path planning (P2) approach [17] which allows for “planned” unmasking and remasking of tokens during the diffusion process. P2 departs from the standard MDM inference procedure in (S7) where the reverse-time i ( x )) is independently sampled for each index by instead assigning transition pr,θ ( xri −1 | xri , Dr,θ r the likelihood of denoising index i as a function of a separate planner GU which represents the probability of unmasking each index. This addresses limitation (1) of the standard denoising process. To address limitation (2), P2 also includes a remasking planner GM which represents the probability of an already unmasked token being first remasked then denoised to (potentially) a new value. This can be described with the following transition kernel, where z ∼ Dθ ( xt r ) is a sampled instantiation from the denoiser, and x̄r represents xr after remasking according to GM .    Cat xi ; αr−1 −αr Gi (z, xr )δ(zi )   r −1 1− αr U i pr,θ ( xri −1 | xr , z) = Cat xi ; (αr−1 −αr )GM (z,xi r ) Di ( x̄r ) r −1 (1−α )(1−Cat( xi ,D ( x̄ ))) θ r xri = m xri ̸= m, in practice, we use a uniform GU and GM , as we find that a GM which depends on the confidence of the Dθ reduces structural diversity.

paragraphs.source: Having described separate processes for structure and sequence, it now remains to combine them to perform cogeneration in Diffusion for Sequence-Structure Co-design (D ISCO). We describe a protein x with L residues with sequence component xseq ∈ V L and backbone structure component xstruct ∈ (R4 ) L as x = ( xseq , xstruct ). A noisy protein with sequence noise seq level r and structure noise level t is then denoted as xr,t = ( xr , xtstruct ). We define the conditional forward process pr,t|0 ( xr,t | x0 ) to factorize over modality seq pr,t|0 ( xr,t | x0 ) := pr|0 ( xr | x0 ) pt|0 ( xtstruct | x0struct ) ) and pt|0 ( xtstruct | x0struct ) are defined similarly to the individual cases i.e. L pr|0 ( xr | x0 ) := ∏ Cat( xr seq ; αr δ( x0 ) + (1 − αr )δ(m)) i =1 and pt|0 ( xtstruct | x0struct ) is defined implicitly through simulation of the forward SDE dxtstruct = f t ( xtstruct )dt + gt dWt , struct xtstruct. =0 = x To reverse this process, we need to define a reverse over both spaces that depends on increments ∆s and ∆t . seq p( xr−∆r ,t−∆t | xr,t ) = pr−∆r ( xr−∆r | xr,t ) pt−∆t ( xtstruct −∆t | xr,t ) where pt−∆t ( xtstruct −∆t | xr,t ) is governed by the reverse-time SDE   dxtstruct = − f t ( xtstruct ) + gt2 ∇ xtstruct log pt ( xr,t ) dt + gt dWt and p( xr−∆r | xr,t ) = ∏iL=1 p( xr−∆r | xr,t ) with These equations govern the dynamics of the reverse process, but we still need to parameterize both the structure score relative to the partially noised xr,t as well as the sequence denoiser relative to the same state. To do this, we use a single model Dθ ( xr,t ). This allows us to perform inference along any trajectory of r, t pairs from (1, 1) to (0, 0) for valid generation up to numerical and learning errors. We use the following generative process given our denoising seq model Dθ with structure output component Dθstruct and sequence logit output component Dθ :    α −α  Cat xri −1 ; r1−−1 αr r (z, xr,t )δ( xri )  ps−∆s ,θ ( xri −∆r | xr,t , z) = (αr−1 −αr ) GiM (z,xr,t )  Cat xri −1 ; i,seq i (1−αr )(1−Cat( xr ,Dθ =m xri ̸= m, (S15) dxtstruct = struct ( x , r, t ) − x struct  r,t struct 2 Dθ dt + gt dWt − f t ( xt) + gt σt2 Finally, it remains to define the training scheme for Dθ . Here, we combine independent losses and noising for each component individually. Specifically, we define independent distributions and losses over separate domains as in (S4) and (S8), but replace the modality-specific denoisers with our joint denoiser Dθ , which operates on a noisy input containing both modalities:  1  1 struct struct 2  dαr Er,t,x∼ pdata xr,t ∼ p(xr,t | x)  ∥ D (x)− x ∥ − r,t θ  σ2  dr · 1 − αr t i =1 =m (S17) These tools provide the necessary theory to perform multimodal training and inference. While theoretically any schedule over r, t can be used, we lock together the two schedules during inference only by defining the schedules in terms of a unifying time variable τ.

paragraphs.source: We trained on the weighted PDB dataset described in AlphaFold 3 with a cutoff date of 202109-30. Data processing, including clustering and filtering, follows the procedures described in Sections 2.5.3 and 2.5.4 of the AlphaFold 3 Supplementary Material. The dataset was obtained by downloading with the Protenix scripts/database/download_training_data.sh script from commit a4059a6894c826b7bb81ba753cbdb89029313244. We sample either single chains or chain-pair interfaces, with interfaces defined as pairs of chains with minimum heavy atom separation less than 5 Å. For r ∈ {chain, interface}, the dataset is weighted as w∝ βr (αprot ∗ nprot + αnuc ∗ nnuc + αligand ∗ nligand ) Nclust with β chain = 0.5, β interface = 1, αprot = 3, αnuc = 3, αligand = 1, and Nclust defined by the Protenix dataset clusters. Cropping is performed per sample, where, as in AlphaFold 3 we use a contiguous crop, a spatial crop, and a spatial interface crop. For detailed algorithms, please refer to Section 2.7 of the AlphaFold 3 Supplementary Material [14]. We crop with the following weights: 20% contiguous cropping, 40% spatial cropping, and 40% spatial interface cropping.

paragraphs.source: While our architecture is broadly based on AlphaFold 3, we make a number of changes to enable protein sequence and structure co-design. Unless otherwise specified, we make use of the AlphaFold 3 architectural components and the interested reader may refer to the AlphaFold 3 Supplementary Information for detailed module-wise algorithms. As in AlphaFold 3, our model takes the form of a conditional diffusion model. However, unlike AlphaFold 3, we remove a number of the conditioning modules and add other domain-specific modules. We begin by removing the Template Module from the architecture as well as the MSA Module as computing MSAs for an ever evolving protein sequence trajectory during inference is computationally intractable. To maintain a source of evolutionary information, we replace the MSA Module with a protein language model, in our case DPLM 650M [13].We present the main model architecture in Algorithm 1. Our tokenization scheme sees each amino acid residue and nucleotide correspond to a single token, while for other molecules, we encode each heavy atom as a separate token. Input features correspond to those used in AlphaFold 3, with the exception of all MSA and template features (see Table 5 in the AlphaFold 3 S.I. [14]). To obtain the initial trunk features, we feed the input features to the InputFeatureEmbedder (Algorithm 2 in AlphaFold 3 S.I.)

paragraphs.source: The core of our model is the Denoiser (Algorithm 1), which iteratively refines the single (si ) and pair (zij ) representations over a series of cycles (Ncycle = 4). We first derive the initial features, inputs { si}, using the InputFeatureEmbedder based on the input features { f ∗ }. The initial cz single and pair representations, sinit ∈ Rcs and zinit i ij ∈ R , are generated via linear projections of the embedded inputs. We enrich the initial pair representations with relative position encodings and token bond features.

paragraphs.source: CrossModalEncode module to update representations based on the current sequence and seq structure states, xl and xlstruct , as well as skip connections. The model then integrates evolutionary information via the PlmModule, our drop-in replacement for the MSA Module of AlphaFold 3 and set to be DPLM 650M [13], updating the pair representations using features from the underlying protein language model. To encode chain breaks as input for the pLM, we insert a length 25 glycine linker between all protein chains. To obtain the single representations, we average the hidden states for each transformer block, while for the pair representations, we concatenate all attention weights for each attention block. Following an update to the single representation si , both representations are processed through the PairformerStack. As we believe that for de novo design we require a lighter pairformer than for typical folding tasks, we reduce the number of blocks in the Pairformer from 48 in AlphaFold 3 to 8. The outputs of the skip skip first cycle are cached as skip connections ({si } and {zij }) for use in subsequent modules. After completing all cycles, a final CrossModalEncode is applied before passing the representations to the DiffusionModule to produce the denoised sequence and structure seq estimates, x̂0 and x̂0struct .

paragraphs.source: To inform the trunk representations with the current trajectory of the diffusion process and to better encourage self-consistency between both sequence and structure modalities, the CrossModalEncode module (Algorithm 2) integrates intermediate sequence and structure states. It first calls the DiffusionModule to obtain a preliminary estimate of the fully denoised seq states, x̂0 and x̂0struct . The noisy and denoised sequences are encoded using the frozen DPLM 650M, while the noised and denoised structures are encoded via a separate structure encoder, in our case a Message-Passing Neural Network following the LigandMPNN architecture [15], yielding seq seq seq seq embeddings u0 , ul , v0 , and vl , respectively. The structure encoder weights are initialized from LigandMPNN weights and subsequently trained end-to-end along with the rest of the weights in the overall model, with the structure encoder embeddings being passed through a final layer norm and linear layer before being added to the single representation. These are subsequently added to the single representation si . Inspired by CarbonNovo [37], a distance map Dij is computed from the step 0 structure estimate x̂0struct using a distance one-hot encoder. This spatial information is injected directly into the pair representation zij .

paragraphs.source: The DiffusionModule (Algorithm 3) dictates the generation of both sequence and structural updates. It begins by conditioning the trunk representations on the current structure noise (t̂) noisy and sequence noise (r̂) via DiffusionConditioning. The noisy structure coordinates, xl, noisy are then scaled to dimensionless vectors with approximately unit variance (rl). To process atom-level geometries, the model utilizes an AtomAttentionEncoder which aggregates atomic features into coarse-grained, sequence-local token representations { ai }. These token representations are then passed through a 24-block DiffusionTransformer featuring full self-attention.

(Coarse-grained models simplify a molecule by grouping atoms into larger units so simulations are cheaper.)

paragraphs.source: Following the transformer, the architecture decodes the token activations back to the atomic level using the AtomAttentionDecoder. This decoder is run twice with different configurations for the sequence and structure outputs, with each configuration being a separately trained AtomAttentionDecoder head: update 1. Structure Update: Run with seq_mode = False to compute coordinate updates (rl). logits 2. Sequence Logits: Run with seq_mode = True to predict sequence logits (xl), where in sequence mode we scatter add the atom-level embeddings back to the token level before finally predicting the sequence logits. update Finally, the predicted positional update rl is rescaled and combined with the input noisy positions using the following schedule equation to yield the output structure xlout : xlout = 2 σdata σ · t̂ update noisy xl + q data rl 2 2 σdata + t̂ σ2 + t̂2

paragraphs.source: The DiffusionConditioning block (Algorithm 4) modulates the input representations based on the current noise levels. The pair representations zij are concatenated with relative position encodings and processed through a 2-layer Transition block. For the single representations si , trunk outputs and input features are concatenated and projected. As the sequence and structure times may be different, we opt to embed them separately to help the model distinguish between sequence and structure noise levels. We embed the structure and sequence noise levels using a FourierEmbedding layer. The structure noise t̂ is embedded logarithmically as 14 log(t̂/σdata ), while the sequence noise r̂ is embedded directly. Both noise embeddings are added to si before it undergoes a 2-layer Transition block.

paragraphs.source: The AtomAttentionDecoder (Algorithm 5) maps the processed token-level features back to the required output space. Token activations ai are broadcast to their constituent atoms and skip summed with the corresponding atom-level skip connections ql . This representation is passed through a 3-block, 4-head AtomTransformer using cross-attention. If the decoder is operating in sequence mode (seq_mode = True), the updated atomic features are aggregated back into token-level representations using a scatter-add operation. The final output ŷl is then generated via layer normalization and a linear projection without biases.

paragraphs.source: All training hyperparameters are detailed in Table S1. The model was trained with distributed data parallel across 32 L40S GPUs for 11 days for 160,000 training steps. We treated any atomic position with probability of being resolved less than 0.9 as unresolved and mask out these atoms (or residues) when evaluating the losses. Dropout is only applied in the Pairformer module, and we fully freeze the pre-trained DPLM 650M weights during training. We use a series of losses to train D ISCO as described below. D ISCO is comprised of 888 million parameters in total, with 235 million trainable parameters.

paragraphs.source: 1: function D IFFUSION M ODULE ( { xl skip }, { xl }, t̂, r̂, { f ∗ }, {si }, {strunk}, {ztrunk}, i ij {si }, {zij }, σdata = 16, catom = 128, catompair = 16, ctoken = 768) # Conditioning inputs 2: { si }, { zij } = D IFFUSION C ONDITIONING (t̂, r̂, { f ∗ }, { si}, {strunk}, {ztrunk}, σdata ) i ij # Scale positions to dimensionless vectors with approximately unit variance..q noisy = xl 2 t̂2 + σdata 4: { strunk}+ = L INEAR N O B IAS ( L AYER N ORM ({si i })) skip 5: { ztrunk}+ = L INEAR N O B IAS ( L AYER N ORM ({zij })) ij # Sequence-local Atom Attention and aggregation to coarse-grained tokens skip skip skip 6: { ai }, { ql }, { cl }, { plm } = ATOM ATTENTION E NCODER (noisy { f ∗ }, {r l }, {strunk}, {zij }, catom , catompair , ctoken i ) ai # Full self-attention on token level. 7: ai += L INEAR N O B IAS ( L AYER N ORM (si )) 8: { ai } ← D IFFUSION T RANSFORMER ({ ai }, { si }, { zij }, β ij = 0, Nblock = 24, Nhead = 16) 9: ai ← L AYER N ORM ( ai ) # Broadcast token activations to atoms and run Sequence-local Atom Attention skip skip skip update 10: {rl} = ATOM ATTENTION D ECODER ({ ai }, {ql }, {cl }, { plm }, seq_mode = False) logits skip skip skip 11: { xl} = ATOM ATTENTION D ECODER ({ ai }, {ql }, {cl }, { plm }, seq_mode = True) # Rescale updates to positions and combine with input positions σ2 σ · t̂ noisy update 12: xlout = 2 data · xl + q data · rl 2 σdata + t̂ 2 2 σ + t̂ data logits 13: return { xlout }, { xl}

paragraphs.source: 1: function D IFFUSION C ONDITIONING (t̂, r̂, { f ∗ }, { si }, {strunk}, {ztrunk}, σdata , cz = 128, i ij cs = 384) # Pair conditioning 2: zij = concat([ztrunk, R ELATIVE P OSITION E NCODING ({ f ∗ })]) ij 3: zij ← L INEAR N O B IAS ( L AYER N ORM (zij )) zij ∈ Rcz 4: for all b ∈ [1, 2] do 5: zij += T RANSITION (zij , n = 2) 6: end for # Single conditioning, t is structure noise, r is sequence noise inputs 7: si = concat([strunk, si]) i 8: si ← L INEAR N O B IAS ( L AYER N ORM (si )) s i ∈ Rc s  1 struct 9: n = F OURIER E MBEDDING 4 log(t̂/σdata ), 256 seq 10: n = F OURIER E MBEDDING (r̂, 256) 11: si += L INEAR N O B IAS ( L AYER N ORM (nstruct )) + L INEAR N O B IAS ( L AYER N ORM (nseq )) 12: for all b ∈ [1, 2] do 13: si += T RANSITION (si , n = 2) 14: end for 15: return {si }, {zij } 16: end function 1: function ATOM ATTENTION D ECODER ({ ai }, { ql }, {cl }, { plm }, seq_mode) 2: # Broadcast per-token activations to per-atom activations and add the skip connection skip 3: ql = L INEAR N O B IAS ( atok_idx(l ) ) + ql 4: # Cross attention transformer skip skip 5: {ql } = ATOM T RANSFORMER ({ql }, {cl }, { plm }, Nblock = 3, Nhead = 4) 6: # Scatter add atom embeddings back to token embeddings if sequence mode 7: if seq_mode then 8: ql = S CATTER A DD (ql , tok_idx(l )) 9: end if 10: # Map to positions update or logits 11: ŷl = L INEAR N O B IAS ( L AYER N ORM (ql )) 12: return {ŷl } 13: end function

paragraphs.source: The losses used for the structure diffusion module are largely the same as those used in AlphaFold 3. We recapitulate them here for convenience. The final structure loss is a weighted combination of the following loss. MSE Loss. The main loss used for the structure portion of our diffusion model takes the form of a weighted aligned MSE loss to the predicted denoised structure. We first perform a rigid alignment of the ground truth structure ⃗xlGT GT-aligned {⃗xl } = weighted_rigid_align({⃗xlGT }, {⃗xlstruct }, {wl }) where the weights wl are defined as is_ligand ligand wl = 1 + f lis_dna αdna + f lis_rna αrna + f l and αdna = αrna = 5 and αligand = 10. The MSE loss is then defined as   1 GT-aligned 2 struct − ⃗xl LMSE = mean wl ⃗xl 3 l

paragraphs.sectionTitle: Notably, we do not take the loss on any atomic positions which are unresolved
paragraphs.source: Smooth LDDT Loss. The Smooth LDDT Loss measures the accuracy of predicted atomic coordinates relative to ground truth structures. For each pair of atoms, we compute the difference between predicted and ground truth pairwise distances, then score each pair using a differentiable approximation of the discrete LDDT thresholds via a sum of sigmoid ε lm ∈ [0, 1]. Pairs are masked by an inclusion radius that is modality-aware: nucleotide atoms (DNA/RNA) are evaluated within 30 Å, while all other atoms are evaluated within 15 Å. The final loss is one minus the masked mean per-pair score, such that a loss of 0 corresponds to perfect coordinate recovery. The Smooth LDDT loss is detailed in Algorithm 6. Full Structure Diffusion Loss. The full structure diffusion loss is a weighted combination of the MSE and smooth LDDT losses with Lstruct = 2 t̂2 + σdata LMSE + Lsmooth_lddt (t̂ + σdata )2 where t̂ is the sampled noise level and σdata is a constant based on the variance of the data (we use σdata = 16). During training the structure noise level t̂ is sampled from σdata · exp(−1.2 + 1.5 · N (0, 1)). Before computing the structure diffusion losses, we apply an optimal ground truth chain assignment as described in Subsection 4.2 of the AlphaFold 3 S.I.

paragraphs.source: While the structure diffusion module is a weighted combination of multiple losses, for the sequence diffusion head, we instead make use exclusively of the masked diffusion loss from (S8), introduced in [16, 38]. In our paper we use a linear schedule αr = 1 − r so that the loss 1 1 r weight dα dr · 1−αr = − r . When computing the loss, we mask out unreliable residues. In particular, we mask out any residues for which any of its constituent backbone atoms have occupancy less than 0.8, any residue for which any backbone atom is unresolved, and any residue which is part 1: function S MOOTH LDDTL OSS ({ xl }, { xlGT }, { f l }, { f lis_rna }) # Compute distances between all pairs of atoms δxlm ← ∥ xl − xm ∥ GT ← ∥ x GT − x GT ∥ 4: δxlm m l 5: # Compute distance difference for all pairs of atoms GT − δx 6: δlm ← δxlm lm h  1 1 7: ε lm ← + sigmoid(1 − δlm ) + sigmoid(2 − δlm ) + 4 sigmoid 2 − δlm i sigmoid(4 − δlm ) 8: # Restrict to bespoke inclusion radius 9: f lis_nucleotide ← f lis_dna+ f lis_rna     is_nucleotide GT < 30 Å f is_nucleotide + δx GT < 15 Å 10: clm ← δxlm 1 − f lm l l 11: # Compute mean, avoiding self term 12: lddt = mean (clm ε lm ) / mean (clm ) 2: 3: l ̸=m l ̸=m return 1 − lddt 14: end function 13: of a histag. We define a residue to be part of a histag if it is part of a consecutive run of 5 or more histidine residues. seq In parameterizing our log denoiser Dθ , we utilize the SUBS parameterization from [16], wherein as the reverse process never predicts a m token, we substitute −∞ in for the logit corresponding to the m token. During training, we sample the sequence time r uniformly from [0, 1]. When masking the sequence, we ensure that all amino acid residue information is not leaked to the model by masking all reference information features in addition to any sequence identity features. Specifically, we replace the reference position, mask and charge features with masked versions of themselves. We set the masked reference position to be the average reference conformer position for each backbone atom, averaged over all 20 standard amino acid residues, while for the masked reference charge and mask, we set them to be identical to those of the glycine backbone atoms.

paragraphs.source: We found that inference techniques are critical for model performance, observing in our experiments that the co-designability metrics can scale from 16% to 88% with the same checkpoint, depending on inference settings. Below, we detail the inference pipeline and progressively introduce each technique responsible for this improvement.

paragraphs.source: Standard masked diffusion inference is limited due to the structure of its reverse process. Specifically, the masked diffusion reverse process requires that once a particular token is unmasked, it retains its value throughout the rest of the inference trajectory. This, however, prevents the model from correcting its mistakes and, for optimal performance, requires the model to have learned perfectly how to generate a particular sequence in every n! possible unmasking orderings. Given limited compute budgets learning, such a model is impractical, and significant performance gains have been observed by allowing a masked discrete diffusion model to self-correct its errors [17, 18, 40]. We similarly find that using standard masked discrete diffusion inference performs poorly. To remedy, this we adopt a random remasking strategy [17] wherein at each sequence time r we uniformly choose r percent of tokens to be masked and 1 − r percent to be unmasked with the full procedure summarized in Algorithm 8. This allows the model to correct mistakes it made during the inference trajectory, especially at critical structural residues such as those of loopy regions of a structure and at regions where the structure shifts from disordered to structured.

paragraphs.source: We observed that the model tends to select particular overconfident residues early on during an inference trajectory, which harms overall co-designability. To remedy this, we applied a novel adaptive temperature scheme for the sequence logits, whereby the distribution of overly confident tokens is flattened early in the inference trajectory.

We also maintain a global logit temperature τr for which we use a step function. global = if r < 0.2 The final logit temperature at time r for token position n is then = τr = τr where the entropy term approaches 0 as the sequence time r → 0. Writing the logits for the nth token induced by the denoiser as { xseq,logits,(n) } we finally obtain the tempered distribution (n) (n) we draw sequence samples from prτ ( xi ) ∝ exp( xseq,logits,(n) /τr ). We find that the application of both these sequence temperature methods in conjunction is critical for performance.

paragraphs.source: Figure S2 illustrates the three noise schedules evaluated as inference settings for joint sequencestructure co-design. The naïve schedule (left) pairs the default AlphaFold 3 structure noise schedule with a linear sequence masking schedule, serving as our baseline. The most codesignable schedule (middle) and most diverse schedule (right) modify the sequence masking trajectory, with the latter employing a mask_rate = t1.5 to concentrate unmasking toward the end of the denoising process, encouraging greater sequence diversity. Consistent with prior works on diffusion-based protein structure design [4], we found that the noise scale λ applied to the structure diffusion process is critical for sample quality—excessively large noise scales 1.0 3500 3000 2500 0.6 2000 0.4 1500 1000 0.8 0.2 500 0.0 0 1.0 0.8 0.6 0.4 0.2 0.0 1.0 0.8 0.6 0.4 0.2 0.0 1.0 0.8 0.6 0.4 0.2 0.0 Time Figure S2. Various noise schedules used as inference settings. The leftmost plot displays a naïve schedule representing the default AlphaFold 3 settings paired with a linear sequence schedule. The middle plot is the schedule used in our most co-designable settings, while the right is our most diverse settings (using mask_proportion = t1.5 for sequence). destabilize generation, while overly small values collapse diversity. In practice, we set λ = 0.1 for all reported experiments, which we found to strike the best balance between structural plausibility and sequence diversity across our benchmark tasks.

paragraphs.source: Conditional generation of proteins in complex with other biomolecules extends naturally from our framework. During inference, the conditioning molecule (e.g. a small-molecule ligand or nucleic acid chain) is provided as an input feature: its reference conformer geometry and bond information are supplied through the standard AlphaFold 3 conditioning features, and its atomic coordinates are allowed to be denoised throughout the reverse diffusion trajectory. The protein sequence and structure are generated jointly in the same setting as above, while the conditioning molecule’s features participate in attention and pairformer computations, allowing the model to shape the protein around the target binding partner (and vice versa). No additional training or fine-tuning is required.

paragraphs.source: Noisy guidance [12] is a guidance mechanism that arises naturally from a multimodal diffusion framework with decoupled noise schedules. We detail the entire noisy guidance algorithm in Algorithm 9. Standard Classifier-Free Guidance (CFG) interpolates between a conditional and unconditional score with guidance strength ω: ωsθ ( xtstruct , t, c) + (1 − ω )sθ ( xtstruct , t, ∅).

paragraphs.source: Noisy guidance exploits the fact that we train with a decoupled noise schedule for sequence and structure to replace the unconditional score with a conditional score evaluated at a partially seq noised condition xρ at noise level ρ > r: seq ωsstruct ( xtstruct , xr , t, r ) + (1 − ω )sstruct ( xtstruct , xρ , t, ρ), θ θ ρ > r.

Noisy guidance can also be applied to unconditional joint generation by adaptively choosing ρ > r as both modalities are being generated simultaneously. For the discrete modality, the guidance interpolation is performed in logit space rather than directly averaging the discrete scores. Concretely, given conditional and unconditional logits ℓcond and ℓuncond , the guided discrete score is seq ← softmax(ω · ℓcond + (1 − ω ) · ℓuncond ) , rather than a geometric average of the softmax outputs as proposed in some prior discrete guidance work. This arithmetic interpolation in logit space has been shown to carry theoretical advantages over direct probability-space averaging [42]. In practice, we find that in continuous space, the magnitude of the conditional score does not match the magnitude of the noised score, or equivalently, the conditional and noised structure denoiser outputs. To remedy this, we take the approach of [43] and rescale the guided structure denoiser output based on the ratio of standard deviations of conditional and noised structure struct , D struct as denoiser outputs Dθ,cond θ,ng struct struct σcond = std( Dθ,cond), σng = std( Dθ,ng) struct struct Dθ,rescaled = Dθ,cond · struct struct struct Dθ,final = ϕ · Dθ,rescaled + (1 − ϕ) · Dθ,cond Similar to the usage of CFG, we only apply noisy guidance within a guidance interval struct , ξ struct ) and r ∈ ( ξ seq , ξ seq ). To enable multimodal noisy guidance, for each modality, t ∈ (ξ min max min max we fix a constant modality and add noise to the other modality before feeding both through the model to produce denoiser predictions on the current timestep and noisy inputs before adjusting the respective score function based on the modality we noised. Finally, we found that setting the noisy sequence and structure times ψseq and ψstruct to constants worked best and performed experiments using this.

paragraphs.source: Inference-time steering enables the adaptation of a single pretrained model to diverse and evolving design objectives at sampling time, without the need for retraining. Desired properties are incorporated directly into the generation process, tilting the distribution of samples at each step. Common guidance approaches rely on heuristic score modifications in diffusion models, which only approximately target conditional distributions and often compromise control over the resulting marginals. In this work, we instead consider the Feynman-Kac Corrector (FKC) framework [21], which enables principled sampling from desired target distributions. In the continuous case, this is done by updating samples using a weighted stochastic differential equation (SDE). The Feynman-Kac PDE describes the time-evolution of the density of samples as ∂ptFK ( x ) g2 = −⟨∇, ptFK ( x )vt ( x )⟩ + t ∆ptFK ( x ) + ptFK ( x )(ht ( x ) − ∂t ht ( x ) ptFK ( x )dx ) , where the first two terms correspond to the Fokker-Planck PDE and the last term is a reweighting PDE, assigning each sample a time-dependent log-weight wt based on the weighting function ht ( x ). In the discrete setting [22], the analogous time-evolution of weighted marginals can be described by weighted Forward Kolmogorov Equations (FKEs) of the form.   ∂ptFK (i ) = ∑ ( At ( j, i ) pt ( j) − At (i, j) pt (i )) + pt (i ) ht (i ) − E pt (i) ht (i ) , ∂t j ̸ =i where At (i, j) is the rate matrix. To sample from ptFK ( x ) using FKC, three general steps are required at every timestep t. We start with a batch of K particles (or samples); at each timestep, we do the following to each particle k: 1. Update its state xt based on the reverse process using Eq. (S16) (continuous) or Eq. (S15) (discrete). 2. Compute its log-weight update log wtk+dt = log wtk + ht ( x k )dt. 3. Normalize the weights and use them to resample the particles in the batch. The state and weight updates are derived based on the target distribution we wish to sample from. In Section A.7.1 and Section A.7.4, we demonstrate two use cases of FKC steering and write the state and weight updates for each.

paragraphs.source: The goal of specificity guidance is to generate protein samples that preferentially bind to a desired ligand (on-target) while avoiding binding to an undesired one (off-target). We formalize this as sampling from the target density. on (1− β) off pSG qt ( x ) β t,β ( x ) ∝ qt ( x ) with β < 0. By setting β < 0, the off-target distribution acts as a repulsive term, encouraging samples that are likely under qon while penalizing those that are likely under qoff . In Section A.7.1, we present FKC - Specificity Guidance (FKC-SG) and define the corresponding state and weight updates to sample from pSG t,β ( x ). This corollary follows from FKC - Geometric Average defined in [21]. FKC-SG off Consider two diffusion models qon t ( x ), qt ( x ) and inverse temperature β < 0. The weighted on (1− β) qoff ( x ) β is SDE corresponding to the repulsion of marginals pSG t t,β ( x ) ∝ qt ( x ) off dxt = gt2 ((1 − β)∇ log qon t ( xt ) + β ∇ log q t ( xt )) dt − f t ( xt ) dt + gt dWt , dwt = 2 gt2 off β( β − 1) ∇ log qon dt t ( xt ) − ∇ log qt ( xt ) In Algorithm 10, we show how this is implemented in our sampling framework. Importantly, since our model does not enforce invariance to rotations and translations, one cannot naively combine the scores from the two distributions since they may not necessarily have the same frame of reference. Thus, we first compute the translation and rotation matrices qoff t ( xt ) to on the space of qt ( xt ) using Kabsch alignment before doing guidance or computing weights. Furthermore, we only do steering on the protein state and use the ligand state for conditioning. In Algorithm 10, we denote operations restricted to the protein or ligand components using struct and x struct , respectively. Similarly, operations corresponding to the on-target and off-target xprot lig states are denoted by xon and xoff . paragraphs.source: We investigate the ability of FKC-SG to generate specific-binding proteins for three sets of on/off-targets. The first pair is amino acids valine (on-target) and proline (off-target); the second is steroids aldosterone (on-target) and cortisone (off-target); the third is biotin (on-target) and pyridoxal 5’-phosphate (PLP, off-target). For each, we sweep over inverse temperatures β ∈ {−0.5, −1.0, −2.0}. Previous work also found that resampling at every step reduced sample diversity significantly due to variance of the weights[21], and so we also searched for the best time point to stop resampling (resample = false when τ ∈ {0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1.0}). Finally, we tried particle batch sizes of K ∈ {2, 4, 8}. We found that β = −0.5 worked best, as well as setting resample = false when τ = 0.6. For the valine/proline pair, we used 4 particles and for the other two pairs, we found 8 particles were better.

paragraphs.source: For each ligand pair, we generated K · 200 protein structures and sequences of length 150 using D ISCO with FKC-SG or D ISCO on its own. We refolded all K · 200 sequences with the on-target using Chai-1 and aligned them with the structure generated by D ISCO. If the complex was designable (i.e. RMSDs of both the ligand centroids and proteins were less than 2 Å), we kept the structure. We then folded each off-target with the sequence and computed the RMSD between the ligand centroids after aligning the complex with the protein folded with the on-target. For each batch of K particles, we kept the sample with the largest ligand centroid RMSD (max reward) for further evaluation (max. 200 samples for each method if each batch had one designable complex). For each protein sequence, we folded it three times with Chai-1 for each of the on/off-targets. For each sequence, we measured the pairwise ligand centroid RMSD between all remaining Chai-1 folds (9 comparisons) and kept the minimum RMSD. A sequence was considered to show separation between the on- and off- targets if this minimum RMSD exceeded a fixed threshold (6 Å). We report the number of such specific sequences for each condition.
